title: Untapped capacity of place-based peer-to-peer resource sharing for community resilience
data_sources: IHME King County life expectancy by Census Tract
Seattle Emergency Hubs / SNAP program documentation

data_scope.study_area: two urban communities in Seattle, Washington State, USA
data_scope.exact_sample_size: not stated in abstract; reported in main text and Supplementary Information

core_conclusion: Communities hold a large untapped reservoir of disaster-relief capacity in everyday neighbor-to-neighbor sharing. Modeling place-based peer-to-peer sharing in two Seattle communities, under a 5-day isolation scenario P2P sharing reduces community resilience loss by 13.4-100%, with on average 22-44 social ties per household sufficing for an 80% sharing rate of surplus resources, making bottom-up sharing a viable complement to top-down disaster response.
why_it_matters: Turns the described phenomenon of disaster mutual aid into an explicit, simulatable model with measurable resilience payoff; reveals a no-new-infrastructure safety margin; gives a concrete social-capital target (22-44 ties) for neighborhood preparedness; and argues to elevate community self-organization to a first-class layer of emergency management.
why_nature: Couples space, people and society into a system-level resilience outcome; offers a transferable framework beyond the two study sites; is strongly policy-translatable for emergency management and equity; and releases aggregated data plus code for reproducibility.
added_value_to_my_research: Mobility-inferred ties replace surveys -> city-scale geosocial resilience maps via multi-source fusion
Network-segregation overlay -> equity diagnosis of sharing, reusing spatial entropy and community detection
P2P sharing recast as 2SFCA/R2SFCA accessibility on a social-plus-spatial catchment
Dynamic person-centric resource flow modeled with agent-based and SIR/Monte-Carlo tools
Physics-informed explainable GNN -> transferable resilience operator with interpretable levers
